mapQuestions.question: Which part of the world is Jamaica in?
mapQuestions.answer: The Caribbean Sea. Jamaica is an island country sitting just south of Cuba and west of Haiti 🌎

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Jamaica?
mapQuestions.answer: Kingston, on the south coast. It is the biggest city, and the cultural and music heart of the country.

mapQuestions.question: How big is Jamaica?
mapQuestions.answer: About 11,000 square kilometres, around half the size of Wales. About 2.8 million people live here.

mapQuestions.question: What is the highest mountain in Jamaica?
mapQuestions.answer: Blue Mountain Peak, 2,256 metres. The misty mountainside grows some of the most expensive coffee in the world.

mapQuestions.question: Who controlled Jamaica before independence?
mapQuestions.answer: Britain, from 1655 to 1962. Jamaica is now an independent country but is still part of the Commonwealth and uses the King of Britain as its head of state.

foods.name: Jerk chicken
foods.description: Chicken marinated in a fiery spice rub (allspice, scotch bonnet, thyme, ginger and more) then slow-cooked over pimento wood on a smoking grill. The national flavour.

foods.name: Ackee and saltfish
foods.description: The national dish. Ackee, a yellow tropical fruit that looks like scrambled eggs once cooked, sautéed with salted cod, onion and pepper. Usually eaten for breakfast.

foods.name: Jamaican patty
foods.description: A flaky golden pastry filled with spiced ground beef. Sold in every bakery and at every petrol station. Eat one on its own, or shove it into a coco bread roll.

foods.name: Rice and peas
foods.description: Rice cooked with coconut milk, kidney beans (called "peas" here), thyme, garlic and scotch bonnet. The Sunday side dish for every Jamaican family.

foods.name: Fresh coconut water
foods.description: Sold by men with machetes on roadsides. They lop the top off a green coconut, stick in a straw, and you drink the sweetest cold water in the world straight from the shell.

foods.name: Bun and cheese
foods.description: A dark spiced fruit loaf eaten in thick slices with a slab of yellow processed cheese. Eaten especially around Easter, but loved year-round.

scavengerItems.label: A Bob Marley image (on a wall, T-shirt, shop sign — they're everywhere)
A huge speaker stack on the street pumping out reggae or dancehall
Rasta colours (red, gold, green) on a flag, hat or wristband
A goat or chicken wandering through a village
The Blue Mountains in the distance, often half hidden by cloud
A man selling coconuts from a wheelbarrow on the side of the road
A Red Stripe beer (the iconic Jamaican lager)
Kids playing cricket in a clearing, on a beach, or in a backyard
A truly perfect Jamaican sunset over the sea
A painted route taxi or minibus crammed with people
A jerk-pit drum smoking on the roadside

geoMatches.description: The capital and biggest city, on the south coast. The heart of reggae and dancehall music, home of the Bob Marley Museum, and gritty street life.
On the far western tip. Seven Mile Beach, cliffs you can jump off into the sea, and some of the most famous sunsets on Earth.
The big tourist resort city on the north coast. Where most cruise ships dock and big all-inclusive hotels line the beaches.
Steep misty mountains in the east. Coffee plantations cling to the slopes, growing Blue Mountain coffee, one of the most expensive coffees in the world.
A wide staircase of waterfalls on the north coast that you can actually climb up, hand in hand with a guide and a long line of other tourists.
A quieter, untouristed stretch of the south coast. Fishing villages, dark golden sand, and a more laid-back vibe than the resorts up north.

senses.smell: Jerk smoke off a drum grill, sea salt and pimento wood, ripe mango on a market stall, ocean breeze, reggae sound system speakers warming up, fresh coconut water…
senses.hear: Reggae bass thudding from a passing car, "Wah gwaan?", roosters crowing at 5am, palm leaves rattling in the breeze, ocean on the rocks, dominos being slammed onto a wooden table…
senses.taste: Hot spicy jerk chicken, sweet ripe mango, fresh coconut water from a green shell, soft sweet bun with sharp cheese, salty ackee, ice-cold Ting (grapefruit fizzy)…
senses.feel: Hot Caribbean sun on bare shoulders, soft white sand under your toes, warm ocean water, sticky humid air, cool Blue Mountain breeze in the evenings, sea spray on cliffs…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Why is reggae played everywhere?
stories.body: Jamaica invented reggae music in the late 1960s, growing out of older styles called ska and rocksteady. The world's most famous Jamaican is Bob Marley, who died in 1981 aged just 36, but whose music plays everywhere in Jamaica every single day. He was the first global music superstar to come out of a poor country. His songs "Three Little Birds", "One Love" and "Redemption Song" are still played at weddings, funerals and political rallies around the world. UNESCO has put reggae music on its list of "intangible cultural heritage of humanity" — recognising how much one tiny island has changed global music.

stories.question: Why are Jamaican runners the fastest in the world?
stories.body: Tiny Jamaica, with only 2.8 million people, keeps producing the world's fastest sprinters. Usain Bolt, the fastest human ever recorded (9.58 seconds for 100m), is Jamaican. So are top women like Shelly-Ann Fraser-Pryce and Elaine Thompson-Herah. Theories: the volcanic-soil farms produce strong-legged children who walk and run up hills from age 3, school sports culture is enormous (the annual "Champs" athletics meet is the biggest event of the year), and there is a strong tradition of athletic talent passed down generations. Whatever the real reason, watch a Jamaican primary-school sports day and you'll spot Olympic-level talent in the under-10s.
stories.thinkingQuestion: Tiny Jamaica beats huge countries at sprinting. What's one thing your own home is unexpectedly great at?

stories.question: Where did "jerk" cooking come from?
stories.body: Jerk is the Jamaican way of cooking meat with a hot spice rub (allspice, scotch bonnet pepper, garlic, thyme, ginger, and lots more) over slow-burning pimento wood. The traditional grill is half a metal oil drum cut open and laid on its side. The technique started with the Maroons — Africans who escaped slavery and lived free in the mountains — cooking meat over slow, almost-smokeless fires that wouldn't give away their hiding places. The slow smoke and intense spice mix turned out to be one of the great inventions of the world. Today you can buy "jerk seasoning" in supermarkets everywhere, but a roadside jerk drum in Jamaica tastes like nothing else.

stories.question: Who were the Maroons, and how did they win their freedom?
stories.body: From the 1600s to 1838 Jamaica was a British sugar colony, built on the forced labour of hundreds of thousands of enslaved Africans. Many escaped into the rugged Blue and John Crow Mountains. These "Maroons" set up their own free communities, growing food, raising children, and fighting guerrilla wars against the British army for decades. Their leaders, including a famous woman called Queen Nanny, won partial independence for the Maroons in 1739. Slavery in the rest of Jamaica was finally abolished in 1838. Today most Jamaicans descend from enslaved Africans. The country's motto is "Out of many, one people", and the Maroon descendants in towns like Accompong still mark their own independence date every January.
stories.thinkingQuestion: A handful of Maroons hidden in the mountains forced an army to give up. What lets a much smaller group beat a much bigger one over time?

stories.question: What happened to the slave-trade money?
stories.body: When slavery was abolished in Jamaica in 1838, the British government paid £20 million in compensation — not to the freed Africans, but to the British plantation owners who had lost their human "property". Britain only finished paying off the loan it took out to do this in 2015. The same plantations that had created huge profits for British families also created the wealth gap that still shapes the Caribbean today. Jamaica is now part of CARICOM, a Caribbean group calling on European nations to pay reparations for slavery. Some royal members of the British family have apologised in person. So far no country has paid reparations to Jamaica. The conversation continues.

convoQuestions.question: Best thing you have eaten in Jamaica so far?
Have you heard real reggae somewhere unexpected yet?
Can you say "Wah gwaan?" with a properly Jamaican accent?
The pace of life feels slower here. Is that nice, or weird?
What's the most "Jamaican" thing you have seen so far?
Jamaica is tiny but punches huge — music, sport, food, identity. How does it do that?

wordSearch.hint: Jamaican foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Free-range goat
animals.description: Everywhere — eating roadside grass, blocking traffic, lounging in shade.

animals.name: Wandering chicken
animals.description: Crossing every village street and clucking at every guesthouse.

animals.name: Yard cat
animals.description: Skinny independent cats around every Jamaican kitchen and beach bar.

animals.name: Anole lizard
animals.description: Bright green lizards doing push-ups on tropical garden walls.

animals.name: Doctor bird (streamertail hummingbird)
animals.description: Jamaica's national bird — black-and-green with long tail feathers, only found here.

animals.name: Land crab
animals.description: On road crossings near the coast, especially after rain.
